food_subgroup: Long-grain aromatic white rice, raw uncooked

ontology.foodex2_description: Rice grain, milled white, long-grain aromatic (basmati)
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for raw basmati rice grain; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient for this record.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard dry serving of basmati rice is approximately ½ cup (90 g raw), which yields roughly 1 cup of cooked rice and provides approximately 320 kcal before cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Rice, white, long-grain, regular, raw, unenriched — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169756)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for white long-grain raw rice used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values align with the input record. Basmati is a long-grain aromatic white rice; USDA SR Legacy does not have a dedicated basmati entry, but the SR Legacy long-grain white rice raw entry is the standard approved proxy and the values are consistent with published basmati composition data.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — Atkinson et al. (2008); University of Sydney GI Database — cooked basmati rice (boiled/absorption method)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 58 is the established value for cooked basmati rice (boiled/absorption method) from the University of Sydney GI Database and corroborated by Atkinson et al. (2008). GI is measured on cooked rice; the raw record inherits this value as the GI is a property of the food after standard preparation. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (58 × 77.6 × 0.9) / 100 ≈ 40.6 for a 90 g dry serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (4 mg/100 g raw)
hypertension.guidance: Basmati rice is very low in sodium at 4 mg per 100 g raw and does not contribute meaningfully to sodium load; it is a safe staple carbohydrate for patients managing hypertension, provided it is prepared without added salt.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index (~58) due to higher amylose-to-amylopectin ratio compared with other white rice varieties
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cooked basmati rice has a GI of approximately 58, meaningfully lower than jasmine rice (GI ~73) or regular white rice (GI ~72–89), making it the preferred rice choice for glycaemic management in patients with type 2 diabetes who consume rice regularly; portion control remains essential given the high carbohydrate density (77.6 g/100 g raw).

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g), negligible cholesterol (0 mg), and low sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Basmati rice is very low in saturated fat (0.1 g/100 g) and contains no cholesterol, making it a heart-neutral staple carbohydrate; it can be included in a cardioprotective diet when consumed in appropriate portions as part of a balanced meal pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Nutritionally neutral; no significant pro-inflammatory or anti-inflammatory properties at standard serving levels
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Basmati rice is nutritionally neutral for rheumatoid arthritis at standard serving sizes and can be consumed freely as a staple carbohydrate without concern for inflammatory exacerbation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Basmati rice is a long-grain aromatic white rice originating from the Punjab and Haryana regions of India and Pakistan. The name 'basmati' derives from the Sanskrit/Hindi word meaning 'fragrant'. Raw basmati grains are characteristically elongated and slender, and upon cooking they elongate further rather than widening — a defining textural property. Basmati has a distinctly lower glycaemic index (GI ~58) compared with jasmine rice (GI ~73) or ordinary white rice (GI ~72–89), attributable to its higher amylose-to-amylopectin ratio, which slows starch digestion. Traditionally, basmati is aged for one to two years post-harvest to develop its characteristic nutty aroma and non-sticky texture. It is the primary grain used in biryani, pulao, and a wide range of Indian and Middle Eastern rice dishes. Per 100 g raw, basmati provides 356 kcal, 8.4 g protein, 0.5 g fat, and 77.6 g carbohydrate, with negligible sodium (4 mg) and saturated fat (0.1 g). Its medium GI makes it the preferred rice choice for patients with type 2 diabetes who consume rice as a dietary staple. It is naturally gluten-free, vegan, and halal-suitable.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, an approved primary source, with exact match to long-grain white rice raw. GI value of 58 is well-established in the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked basmati rice. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are fully populated from the verified input record consistent with the approved source.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for basmati rice is not individually reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry used; total fat is confirmed at 0.5 g/100 g. water_g set to -1: moisture content for raw basmati rice not reported in the primary source entry used. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — rice grain contains no industrially produced trans fats). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-derived grain food). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — milled white rice contains negligible vitamin C). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central — milled white rice contains no significant vitamin A). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-derived food contains no vitamin B12). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-derived grain food contains no vitamin D). Glycemic load per serving calculated for 90 g dry serving: (58 × 77.6 × 0.9) / 100 ≈ 40.6. GI is a property of cooked basmati rice; the raw record inherits this value as GI is measured after standard preparation. SNOMED CT code not identified for raw basmati rice grain; FoodOn (FOODON_03301726) and FoodEx2 (A00GH) anchors are sufficient.
